title: Abstract
content: Thin films of pure or doped nanocrystalline titania have been deposited on glass slides by using a sol-gel procedure, in the presence of surfactant Triton X-100, which acts as template of the nanostructure.Fe 3 , Cr 3 and Co 2 were used as dopants while the doping extended in a broad domain from very low to very high levels.The photocatalytic efficiency of pure or doped titania was tested for discoloration of an aqueous solution of Basic Blue 41.The presence of dopants resulted in a progressive loss of total crystallinity, some transition from anatase into rutile and, in the case of Co 2 , formation of the mixed oxide cobalt titanate.Loss of anatase had dramatic consequences on photocatalytic efficiency by UV-vis excitation, which decreased fast with increasing dopant concentration.Selected visible excitation of the doped titania could lead to photodegradation of the dye but to a far lesser degree than UV-vis excitation.Photosensitization by absorption of light by the dye itself loses its importance in the presence of the dopant.Thus the doped material is a visible-light photocatalyst but substantial photodegradation efficiency is achieved only at very high doping levels, for example, 20 at. for Fe 3 doping.In any case, direct UV excitation of pure titania is a more efficient photocatalytic process than visible excitation of the doped semiconductor.

title: Experimental
content: All reagents used were purchased from Aldrich, were of the best quality available and were used as received. Nanocrystalline titania films were deposited on glass slides (both sides applicable) under the following procedure which is systematically employed in our laboratory (cf.Refs.11,30): 1.4 g of the non-ionic surfactant Triton X-100 polyoxyethylene- (10) isooctylphenyl ether was mixed with 7.6 ml ethanol.Then we added 1.36 ml glacial acetic acid (AcOH) and 0.72 ml of titanium isopropoxide under vigorous stirring.Selforganization of the surfactant in this original sol creates organized assemblies that act as templates defining nanoparticle size.The surfactant is burned out during calcination.Finally, when needed, the appropriate amount of metal salt was added as pre-weighed powder.The following salts have been used: Doping in this work is expressed as at. with respect to Ti.After a few minutes stirring, a glass slide, which was previously thoroughly washed, sonicated in ethanol and dried in a N 2 stream, was dipped in the above sol and withdrawn at a speed of 50 mmmin by using a dipping device under ambient conditions.After leaving the film to dry in air for a few minutes, it was calcined in an oven.The temperature was raised at a pace of 208min to 550 8C and then it was left for about 10 min at that temperature.When the film was taken out of the oven it was transparent and optically uniform.The mass of titania on the slide was approximately 100 mg (that is about 50 mg on each side of the slide).In the case of X-ray diffraction (XRD, see below) measurements, such small quantity of titania does not suffice to record a readable diffractogram.Therefore, in that case, multiple dippings (10 in total) were made following calcination after each dipping as above.For the sake of comparison, XRD characterization was also carried out on pure or doped titania in powder form.The powder was obtained from the original sol by vacuum evaporation of the solvent followed by calcination, as in the case of films themselves. Photodegradation experiments were carried out in a small cylindrical batch reactor containing about 4 ml of an aqueous solution of the organic substance to be photodegraded.Inside the reactor we fit the glass slide with titania film in such a way that all part of the slide bearing catalyst was immersed in the solution.The reactor was illuminated by a 450 W xenon lamp.For the shake of comparison, some experiments were made by introducing titania powder into the reactor.Illumination was carried out by continuous stirring, the rest of the above conditions being the same.Only one side of the slide was phasing the lamp but since the films were transparent, light was absorbed by both film layers.In order to eliminate the UV emitted by the lamp, we used a cut off filter at 400 nm.The intensity of light at the position of the slide was 118 mWcm 2 without filter and 107 mWcm 2 with UV cut off filter and it was regulated by using multiple wire grids.Intensities were measured with an Oriel 70260 Radiant Power meter.The solution discoloration was monitored my absorption spectrophotometry at regular time intervals. UV-vis absorption measurements were made with a Varian Cary 1E spectrophotometer and X-ray diffraction measurements (XRD) with a Philips PW 1840 diffractometer while atomic force microscopy (AFM) images were obtained with a digital instruments microscope in the tapping mode.

title: Results and discussion
content: In order to study the photocatalytic degradation of an aqueous organic load we have used a model dye, namely Basic Blue 41 (BB).The structure of BB can be seen in the inset of Fig. 1.We have chosen this dye because we have repeatedly employed it in the past 6,11,30313233 and because it fits the present purpose.The titania films made by the above procedure adsorb a high load of the dye 11 and for this reason photodegradation is rapid.The BB concentration in the aqueous solution used for photodegradation was 10 A5 M and its pH was 6.5.Since BB, which is a cationic molecule, is strongly adsorbed by the present titania films it is obvious that the isoelectric point of these films lies below this pH value.The isoelectric point of pure titania is known to be around 5.8-6.0 1,34 but it may take substantially higher values depending on the method of preparation, doping, etc. 19,34,35.FTIR absorption spectra, not shown, of similar films deposited on silicon wafers show that the employed synthesis procedure produces nanocrystalline titania with a relatively large concentration of surface hydroxyl groups.The marked photocatalytic efficiency of these films, which has been previously established and which will also emerge in the present data is, among other reasons, also due to the high surface hydroxyl content.As already said, hydroxyl groups scavenge photogenerated holes thus preventing electron-hole recombination, at the same time producing highly oxidant hydroxyl radicals 2,15,36, which lead to dye photodegradation.In other words, for the purposes of the present work we employ nanocrystalline titania, deposited on a flat substrate as a thin film, proven to be an efficient photocatalyst and a photodegradable dye proven to fit an efficient photodegradation scheme.The discoloration of the aqueous solution was monitored by measuring absorbance at the BB absorption maximum (see Fig. 1, for absorption spectra of BB and of pure titania films). Titania has been deposited on glass slides and it was characterized by AFM, XRD and UV-vis, always in film geometry.Both pure and doped titania films have nanocrystalline structure.This is immediately seen by inspecting AFM images of Fig. 2 and it is also deduced by studying the corresponding X-ray diffractograms of Fig. 3. Nanostructure in these films was dictated by self-organization of surfactant template.Pure titania consists of anatase nanocrystals.The nanoparticle size calculated through the AFM images was 15-20 nm.The corresponding nanoparticle size calculated through XRD by using the Scherrer formula 11 was 17 nm.The closeness of these values is an indication of the extent of titania nanocrystallinity in the present films.Titania was doped with three characteristic transition metal ions, i.e.Fe 3 , Cr 3 and Co 2 .These dopants have been chosen because they are popular among several researchers and because they serve the purposes of the present work.Doping extended from very low to very high levels in order to let emerge all related issues.The presence of these ions induced a change in the nanocrystallinity of the films, which consisted no more of pure anatase but they were a mixture with rutile while, at sufficient dopant concentration, the only detected nanocrystalline phase was rutile.The low relative intensity of the rutile peaks suggests that most probably what happens is not transition of anatase into rutile but simply partial loss of crystallinity with simultaneous partial rutile formation.Fig. 3a-c reveals the progressive loss of anatase and formation of rutile in the case of Fe 3 -doping.Rutile was detected at high dopant concentration but decrease of anatase peak intensity was progressive.This behavior is not a characteristic of the film geometry.Similar results were obtained also with doped titania powder.It is interesting to note that, even though high iron content has been introduced, no ferric oxides have been detected by XRD.This is in accordance with previous findings 373839.The evolution was faster in the case of Cr 3 .The presence of the dopants induced also a variation of the nanoparticle size.The AFM images of Fig. 2 show that the size of the nanoparticles increased in the case of chromium from 15-20 nm to 30-35 nm.On the contrary, in the case of cobalt the size decreased to 12-15 nm.In the case of iron no variation of the nanoparticle size was detected.As seen in Fig. 3, in all three types of doped titania the most intense rutile XRD peak appears at about 548, which corresponds to the (2 1 1) crystallographic plane 40.No variation of the position of the peaks was detected when the concentration of the dopant varied.This is a rough indication that dopant ions either occupy interstitial sites within the nanocrystal lattice or that they substitute Ti 4 but without important distortion of the lattice.The diffractogram of cobalt-doped titania has a particularity that differentiates it from the other two cases.In that case, additional XRD peaks appear, which are identified with cobalt titanate (CoTiO 3 ) 41,42.We can conclude from these data that while Co 2 -doped titania produces cobalt titanate and rutile, in the other two cases the presence of the dopants simply induces loss of crystallinity and limited formation of rutile nanocrystals.The highest percentage of rutile was observed in the case of Co 2 .Data previously reported by other researchers and different preparation methods describe similar structural features for titania doped by the above cations 16,17,373839424344.For example, Ref. 16 presents a phase diagram showing amorphous phase extent and crystalline-phase transitions for iron-doped titania while Refs.38,39 show anatase phase loss in the presence of Fe 3 and Ref. 43 in the presence of chromium ions.Loss of crystallinity or formation of rutile has its consequences on photocatalytic efficiency as it will be seen below. The principal motive of doping titania with transition metals or other dopants is to extent titania light absorption into the visible.This goal has been reached in all previous works and it has also been reached in the present work as it is seen in Figs.456.Doped titania does absorb in the visible in all three cases studied.As expected, the absorbance in the visible increases with the concentration of the dopant.The presence of ferric ions results (Fig. 4) in a large red shift of the absorption onset.This spectral behavior is indicative of the preservation of the semiconductive nature of the Fe 3 -doped material, even though, comparison of the film absorption spectra with the absorption of Fe(NO 3 ) 3 in aqueous solution (see inset of Fig. 4) might cause some uncertainty.Obviously, the spectral red shift originates from excitation of 3d electrons from ferric ions into the conduction band of the semiconductor 17.The spectral behavior of titania films doped with Fe 3 is similar to that observed in previous works 17,20,22.On the contrary, the presence of Cr 3 ions resulted in a structured spectrum (i.e. a characteristic band around 400 nm) and not in a simple red shift of the absorption onset, which is typical of semiconductor's behavior.When Cr 3 is dissolved in water, it has an absorption spectrum dominated by two characteristic absorption bands located around 400 and 580 nm (cf.inset of Fig. 5 and Ref. 3).It is obvious that the short wavelength band of the spectrum of the solution is identified with the characteristic absorption band of the film (Fig. 5).Obviously, this is not a transition from a Cr 3 ion state lying within the band gap of titania to its conduction band but it is a transition localized within the ion itself.Some previously published data present a different UVvis behavior for Cr 3 -doped titania 3,454647 thus underlining the importance of the synthesis method used for the doping process.Co 2 doping produced an impressive red shift of the absorption onset within the visible spectrum and an additional broad absorption band at longer wavelength (cf.absorption spectrum of Co(NO 3 ) 2 in aqueous solution in the inset of Fig. 6).These spectral characteristics are in general agreement with previously published data 20,44.We may then conclude that the synthesis procedure employed in the present work preserved the semiconductive capacity of the nanocrystallites and red shifted the absorption onset only in two cases: Fe 3 and Co 2 doping.Cr 3 seems to be simply ''dissolved'' in titania.to the percentage of photodegraded BB during a given total irradiation time, which was set at 90 min for all samples.In reality, as already said, the height of the bars corresponds to the extent of discoloration of the treated BB aqueous solution.Since all original solutions were identical, the histogram then presents the efficiency of photodegradation as a function of the dopant concentration.Doping started at small dopant concentration and ended at very large percentages with respect to Ti. UV-vis irradiation can affect photodegradation by three different pathways: (1) direct band gap excitation of titania by absorption of UV photons, which is considered as the main source of photocatalysis; (2) excitation of the doped nanocrystallites in the visible; (3) photosensitization of titania by visible excitation of the dye and electron transfer into the conduction band of the semiconductor 48,49.The extent of photosensitization of titania by BB has been studied in separate experiments using pure titania catalyst (cf.Ref. 6) and it was found to offer an important contribution to BB photodegradation.However, it does not seem to play a crucial role in the present analysis.In all three studied cases, the efficiency of UV-vis photodegradation dramatically decreased in the presence of dopants.The decrease was faster in the case of Fe 3 and Co 2 dopants and slower in the case of Cr 3 (compare Figs. 7 and9 with Fig. 8).This difference may be due to the fact that Cr 3 is not a real dopant of titania in the present case, as seen in the previous paragraph.In view of the discussion in the Section 1 and in the previous paragraphs, we can propose that the decrease of photodegradation efficiency in the presence of dopants may be due to the following reasons: (1) electron-hole recombination at dopant sites; (2) loss of crystallinity in the presence of dopants; (3) formation of rutile at the expense of anatase; (4) other possible reasons such as population of lowerlying dopant energy states and decrease of electron reductive capacity.Literatures are very rich on the subject of metal-ion doping of titania 1235,1617181920212236373839434445464750,51, even though, no standard model has been established.Some researchers support the idea that cationic dopants of higher valence than Ti 4 increase photocatalytic efficiency while cationic dopants of lower valence (like the present ones) have adverse effects on photocatalysis 5,51.Others distinguish altervalent cations into those that do enhance photocatalytic efficiency of titania, e.g.W 6 5,20,51, and those, which have adverse effects, like V 5 20,50.For lower valence dopants, contrary to the majority of results, there are also reports on photoreactivity enhancement.For example, in Ref. 22 most cationic dopants studied enhanced photoreactivity of quantumsize titania.This finding can be justified by the fact that in that case titania nanoparticles were so small that photogenerated charge carriers could rapidly reach the particle surface 45 and they could thus be rapidly scavenged diminishing risk of recombination.As a matter of fact, the different synthesis procedures used each time to synthesize doped titania and the different types of substances used for photodegradation studies create a varying set of data that can frequently become controversial.In conclusion, the present data show that all three cationic dopants studied had a detrimental effect on photocatalysis by UV-vis irradiation.It is obvious that UV-vis excitation of titania by using a xenon lamp means that photocatalysis functions by direct Near-UV excitation of titania (cf.spectrum of Fig. 1). Visible excitation of pure and doped titania gave an interesting set of data (light bars) that describe a different picture from the previous one, especially in the case of ferric doping.Indeed, Fig. 7 shows that photodegradation efficiency decreased by increasing dopant concentration up to 0.5 at..At higher dopant concentration the efficiency increased achieving a maximum at 20 at..This finding was verified in many different preparations.This result indicates that within a certain doping range it is possible to synthesize Fe 3 -doped titania that does act as an important photocatalyst in the visible range.The small visible response of pure titania is obviously due to photosensitization.Photosensitization is diminished and possibly nullified in the presence of the dopant indicating that at sufficient dopant concentration what matters is only absorption of light by the doped semiconductor.This is in accordance with previous findings 17.A similar behavior was recorded with Cr 3 and Co 2 -doped titania (Figs. 8 and9), even though, the effect was less pronounced in those cases.Among the three cations, only Fe 3 offered doped semiconducting nanocrystallites with some interesting photocatalytic behavior in the visible. Finally, it should be noted that, even though the absolute rates were different, comparative data obtained by using pure or doped titania in the form of powder did not affect the general conclusions obtained by studying titania films.

title: Conclusions
content: Pure and doped nanocrystalline titania films can be easily synthesized by using a straight forward sol-gel method.Three cationic dopants have been studied: Fe 3 , Cr 3 and Co 2 while doping level ranged in a large domain.In all cases, the films, which have been deposited on glass slides, are nanostructured, as a result of the presence of surfactant Triton X-100 in the original sol.Pure titania consisted of anatase nanocrystals of about 15-20 nm particle diameter.The presence of the ions caused variation of the size of the nanocrystals, partial loss of crystallinity, phase transition from anatase to rutile and, in the case of Co 2 , creation of the mixed oxide cobalt titanate.No single or mixed oxides involving iron or chromium have been detected.All doped materials strongly absorbed light in the visible.The absorption spectra suggest that the presence of ferric ions gives a material behaving as a doped semiconductor, i.e. having energy states located within the titania band gap.Co 2 doping led to a material with mixed behavior involving both doped titania and cobalt titanate.Cr 3 doping gave a principally amorphous material simply dissolving metal ions.Doping in all cases had a detrimental effect on the photocatalytic efficiency of titania.Nevertheless, doping with ferric ions has some interest as visible light photocatalyst if a rather large doping level is achieved (20 at.). As a general conclusion, the above data suggest that pure nanocrystalline titania is a better photocatalyst than doped titania.Even though, absorption in the visible is an attractive quest, it is preferable to employ an installation with inexpensive UV lamps, like black-light tubes 30 and inexpensive methods of nanocrystalline titania deposition, like the present one, than to rely on questionable doping with the hope to use solar radiation.Loss of crystallinity or transition from anatase to rutile, which seems to be an immediate consequence of doping, is not an encouraging situation. Fig. 1.Absorption spectrum of (a) pure titania and (b) a 10 A5 M aqueous solution of Basic Blue 41.Inset: the chemical structure of the dye. Fig. 2. AFM images of pure and doped titania films. Fig. 3. XRD spectra of pure and doped titania films. Figs. 7-9 show photodegradation data for 10 A5 M aqueous solutions of BB in the presence of pure or doped titania films.All three histograms contain two types of data, i.e. data obtained by illumination with both UV and visible light (dark bars) and with only visible light (light bars).The height of each bar corresponds Fig. 4. Absorption spectra of pure and Fe 3 -doped titania films.Inset: absorption spectra of (a) 10 A6 M and (b) 10 A5 M aqueous solutions of Fe(NO 3 ) 3 . Fig. 6.Absorption spectra of pure and Co 2 -doped titania films.Inset: absorption spectrum of a 2 A 10 A6 M aqueous solution of Co(NO 3 ) 2 . Fig. 8. Percentage of photodegradation of BB as a function of Cr 3 concentration.Dark bars: UV-vis excitation; light bars: visible excitation. P. Bouras et al. Applied Catalysis B: Environmental 73 (2007) 51-59 We thank the European Social Fund (ESF), Operational Program for Educational and Vocational Training II (EPEAEK II), and particularly the Program HERAKLITOS, for funding the above work.
